source_metadata.title_raw: Amy Webber - A-18-265 - January 9, 2018 - Long Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Amy Webber 
Deputy City Attorney 
333 West Ocean Blvd., 11th Floor 
Long Beach, CA 90802-4664 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-265 
Dear Ms. Webber: 
January 9, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Long Beach 
Councilmember Suzie Price regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act"). 1 Because your inquiry is general in nature as to the items in Question 2, below, we are 
treating this portion of the request as one for informal assistance.2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Councilmember Price from designating 
her councilmember discretionary funds to defray costs incurred by the Belmont Shore 
Business Association for televising the local holiday parade where her spouse's business is a 
member of the Association and his business is located on the Second Street parade route? 
2. As a general matter, what issues are involved in future decisions affecting either the Long 
Beach Chamber of Commerce or businesses located with 500 feet of the Councilmember's 
husband's business? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329( c )(3).) 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-18-265 
Page No. 2 
1. The facts provided do not indicate there is a reasonably foreseeable and material financial 
effect on the Councilmember's interests such that she is barred from participating in this 
decision. 
2. Please review the general information provided below. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER3 
Councilmember Suzie Price represents the City of Long Beach's Third Council District, 
which is home to several business districts. Her spouse recently bought into an eyelash extension 
franchise, and established the first store on Second Street in the Councilmember' s district. The 
business was formed as a corporation and is community property. The store occupies retail space on 
Second Street subject to a seven-year lease with a value of $5,760 for the first year, increasing in 
value in subsequent years. 
All businesses within this geographic corridor must join the Belmont Shore Business 
Association ("Association"). The Association is a nonprofit group which works to improve 
conditions for businesses on Second Street by providing items such as publicity and special events. 
_There are approximately 281 businesses registered as members of the Association. Business owners 
pay annual membership fees to participate in these activities. Second Street is in a popular coastal 
entertainment and dining area of the City, and is home to many retail and service businesses. 
One of the Association's activities is the Belmont Shore Christmas Parade, an annual 
evening event, which draws an estimated 40,000 visitors who-eat, drink and shop at facilities 
located along the parade route on Second Street. The parade is privately funded by the Association, 
through local sponsorships. 
The 2018 parade occurred earlier in December; however, the Association had a shortfall in 
funding and requested assistance in paying for its television coverage of the event. The entire event 
was broadcast on the local city television channel and available for streaming on the internet. The 
coverage showed floats, community groups, and public officials passing in front of the storefronts 
located a few blocks from her spouse's business. The coverage did not feature her spouse's 
business. Councilmember Price wishes to use her council-apportioned discretionary fund in the 
amount of $2,500 to help defray the Association's broadcast coverage costs. Because this is a 
special event not already under the budget authorization of the City Council, this use of her 
discretionary fund is subject to the approval of the City Council. 
Councilmember Price anticipates there will be future decisions that could also implicate her 
financial interests related to her spouse's business. For example, her spouse intends to become a 
member of the Long Beach Chamber of Commerce, a nonprofit association that represents the 
business interests in the City. The Chamber relies on membership fees for its operations, and 
3 These facts were presented in the initial request, and supplemented by information provided by telephone and 
emails on December 18, 2018. 


File No. A-18-265 
Page No. 3 
receives no City funds, but does appear before the City Council on occasion to advocate for 
its members' interests. She also anticipates future development applications and business permits 
that may involve businesses or property located within 500 feet of her spouse's business. The 
Councilmember seeks general guidance on these matters. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in 
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial 
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any of the interests specified in Section 87103. The financial interests 
implicated by your facts are: 
• 
A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is 
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of 
management (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A real property interest, where an official has a direct or indirect interest of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real 
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate 
family owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent interest or greater 
(Section 82033). 
• 
An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or 
more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) This 
includes any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata 
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his or 
her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO-percent or greater 
interest. (Section 82030(a).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's 
immediate family. This is known as "the personal financial effects" rule. (Section 
87103.) 
Presuming that the financial minimums above are met, Councilmember Price has 
a business entity interest in her spouse's eyelash extension franchise business, a real 
property interest in the business's lease, and a source of income interest in the business. If 
income derived from any particular client or customer aggregates to $500 or more in the 
12 months prior to the decision at issue, Councilmember Price will also have a financial 


File No. A-18-265 
Page No. 4 
interest in the client or customer as a source of income, unless the exception for income 
from retail sales applies. 4 
Question 1: Belmont Shores Business Association Parade Funding Decision 
The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Councilmember Price's business, lease and source of income interests are 
not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue, in that none of these interests are the subject of, or 
named as a party in, this decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial 
effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Business Entity and Source of Income Interests 
Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standard for interests in business entities, 
including business entities that are sources of income to the official.5 For a business entity not 
explicitly involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.1 (b) establishes that a foreseeable effect of a 
decision is material if "a prudent person with sufficient information" would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect "would contribute to a change in the value of a 
privately-held business entity." Examples of applicable decisions include those that: 
(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity 
in which the business entity is engaged; 
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business entity is engaged; 
(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business 
entity supplies; 
( 4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment 
projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either 
temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives; 
(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other 
project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws 
from the location of the project; or 
4 Section 87103.S(a) provides an exception for a retail customer if the retail customers of the business entity 
constitute a significant segment of the public generally, and the amount of income received by the business entity from 
the customer is not distinguishable from the amount of income received from its other retail customers. 
5 Where the source of income in a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 applies the standards for materiality in 
Regulation 18702.1. 


File No. A-18-265 
Page No. 5 
(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the 
business entity. 
(Regulation 18702.l(b).) 
Under the facts provided,·it is arguable that providing the discretionary funds to the Belmont 
Shores Business Association may assist the Association's budget and allow the Association to 
better assist it's member businesses with future promotions and events. However, considering the 
limited purposes of the decision and the fact that discretionary funds amount to only $2,500, the 
facts presented would not cause a prudent person to find it would foreseeably contribute to a change 
in the value of the business. Therefore, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will 
materially affect the Councilmember's interest in her spouse's business. As noted above, no clients 
or customers were identified for analysis under the source of income materiality criteria in 
Regulation 18702.3.6 
Real Property Interest: Leasehold 
For a leasehold interest, Regulation 18702.2(b) establishes that a foreseeable effect of a 
decision is material if the decision will do any of the following: change the termination date, change 
the potential rental value, change the official's use (legal or actual), or impact the use and 
enjoyment of the leased property. Again, considering the limited purposes of the decision and the 
relatively small amount of funds, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will materially 
affect the Councilmember's leasehold interest. 
Accordingly, Councilmember Price's financial interests would not bar her from participating 
in this funding decision. 
Question 2: Other Decisions 
Long Beach Chamber of Commerce 
You have requested general guidance on future decisions that may involve the Long Beach 
Chamber of Commerce, a private organization that acts to support local businesses. For a decision 
of this type, the Councilmember will need to consider her financial interests involved in the 
particular decision; and whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on any of her financial interests, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on these interests. Please contact our office for additional advice when a specific decision 
implicates her financial interests. 
Oiher Properties near the Business Entity 
You have requested general guidance where a decision may involve a business within 500 
feet of the Councilmember's business interest. Similarly, the Councilmember will need to 
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the particular decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on her financial interests 
6 In the event that a client or customer is implicated by this decision, you would need to seek further advice. 


File No. A-18-265 
Page No. 6 
including the business entity and source of income interests, as well as her real property interest 
identified in the facts presented. Note that this determination must be made for any decision 
implicating her spouse's business or leasehold interest regardless of whether a future decision 
involves a business located within 500 feet of the Councilmember' s business. Regulations 18702.1, 
18702.2 and 18702.3 provide the relevant materiality standards for each type of the 
Councilmember's currently identified interests (business entity, source of income and real 
property). Please contact our office for additional advice in the case that a decision implicates the 
Councilmember' s financial interests. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
ti::Jl~ 
Counsel, Legal Division

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329( c )(3).)
